---
title: "3 Best Houseplants for All Seasons"
description: "Discover the best houseplants that thrive beautifully in every season of the year"
date: 2026-06-15
categories: [houseplants, indoor-garden]
image: "../../images/Snake_Plant.webp"
author: "Rachel"
draft: false
toc: true
---

I've been growing plants since I was about nine years old. Over the years, I've learned just how much climate affects a plant's health. But when I started exploring indoor gardening, I quickly discovered that it comes with its own challenges. Space, safety around children and pets, and even your decorating style can affect which plants will thrive.

Even so, climate remains one of the most important factors. Plants that can handle a range of temperatures and humidities are far more likely to stay healthy throughout the year.

In this article, I share three houseplants that I've found to be reliable through every season and explain why they might be a good fit for your home.

## My Checklist for Choosing Hardy Houseplants

**What does my home environment look like?** 
 
 Before bringing a plant home, I think about the space and size of my house. I live minimally and love big open spaces, so, I'll be looking for plants that don't take up too much of my space. That naturally rules out plants like Monstera deliciosa and the Parlor Palm. Although it is easy to style to take up less space, I classify the Devil's Ivy in this category too because of its aggressive linear growth. I've seen pictures online of indoor spaces turned into green jungles, not that I have anything against green jungles (I think they're great!), but when I think of the bugs that may come with those jungles, my skin literally crawls. 
 
 Other things that come to mind have to do with the climate: lighting conditions, humidity, and temperature. Do I have windows or glass patio doors that can let in light for my plants? What of the airflow indoors? Stagnant humid indoor air creates a breeding ground for all kinds of bugs, including mealybugs and spider mites. I have contact dermatitis that flares up with exposure to these bugs, so, yes, that's worth looking into. 
 
 Overall, take care to understand the climatic variations within your house and choose plants that can tolerate those variations.
 

**Can the plant match my lifestyle?** 
 
 I have a super busy lifestyle, but I always create time for my garden every single day, unless I'm away from home. And that time is important because a plant's like a child. It needs caring, else you'll not notice small things like it's not getting enough light or it's having too much water or it's overgrown its pot.
 
 And that's where hardy plants come in because many of them can tolerate considerable neglect, including little watering and pruning. So you should ask yourself whether time's an issue for you.


**What about safety?** 

 Alright, now this one's super important. In my case, pets and children are non-issues. But if you have either, you must think about how safe Have pets? Children? Go for non-poisonous plants or find safe spaces to display your plants.

## Best All-Season Houseplants

### 1. Spider Plant

The spider plant prefers moderate humidity (40%-60%) and temperature (60°F-75°F), but it can tolerate temperatures as low as 50°F. It can withstand little watering because its thick roots store nutrients and water.

With the spider plant, space is rarely a concern because it can grow up to 60 cm high and spread to about 90 cm when mature. Its foliage, however, can become crowded over time and may require pruning to keep tidy. Although non-toxic, keep it away from pets, as overeating it can cause stomach upset.

### 2. ZZ Plant

The ZZ plant thrives in temperatures between 60°F and 80°F and humidity levels of 40%-50%. It has thick roots for storing water and waxy leaves to prevent unnecessary water loss. Because it grows slowly in an upward direction, it’s ideal for small spaces. However, place it in a safe place, as it is moderately toxic.

### 3. Snake Plant

The snake plant thrives in moderate to high temperatures (60°F-80°F) and low to moderate humidity (60°F-80°F). It has a dense root network and thick, fleshy leaves that store water for use in dry conditions.

The snake plant grows upward to reach a height of about 120 cm when mature. So, it’s perfect for small spaces. For safe growing, however, take care to place it out of reach of children and pets, as it is mildly toxic.

## Final Thoughts

The three plants in this article are survivors, with the ability to grow in the toughest conditions. With a little tender, loving care you can give them a home to thrive in, and we can assure you that your love will be reciprocated.
